planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; in zone 9 start seeds in January for March transplanting. Jet Star is an indeterminate hybrid that bears over a long season and is well-suited to staking or caging in full sun.

directions: Start seeds 6–8 weeks before transplanting at 70–80°F; Jet Star is a popular indeterminate hybrid tomato introduced by Harris Seeds in the late 1960s that earned a lasting following among home gardeners for its combination of large, smooth, red, globe-shaped fruits (8–12 oz) with distinctively mild, low-acid flavor that appeals to people who find standard tomatoes too tart — the low-acid character makes Jet Star the variety of choice for those who love the fresh tomato experience without acid sensitivity. The smooth, crack-resistant fruits have few seeds and thick walls, making them excellent slicing tomatoes for sandwiches and salads; they also hold up well in cooking but are really at their finest fresh off the vine at warm room temperature. VF disease resistance (Verticillium and Fusarium wilt races 1 and 2) keeps plants productive through the season. Jet Star is a mid-season tomato (70–75 days from transplant) and bears continuously as an indeterminate variety — plants will need staking or caging as they can reach 5–6 feet. An excellent choice for gardeners who prefer mild, low-acid tomatoes, and a reliable producer in zone 8–10 spring and fall tomato seasons.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tomato Production Guide
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Tomato Variety Selection for Texas

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Heinz 1350 is a determinate canning tomato that ripens a concentrated crop over 3–4 weeks, ideal for processing in large batches — time transplanting to allow harvest before peak summer heat in zone 9.

directions: Start seeds 6–8 weeks before transplanting at 70–80°F; Heinz 1350 is the classic determinate processing tomato developed by the H.J. Heinz Company, once widely grown commercially for ketchup production, that became a home garden favorite for anyone serious about canning, sauce-making, or tomato processing — it produces a concentrated set of uniform, medium-sized (5–6 oz), globe to slightly blocky, bright-red fruits with thick, meaty walls, small seed cavities, and very firm texture that holds up exceptionally well to cooking and reduces beautifully to rich tomato sauce without excessive water. The determinate compact plants (3–4 feet) produce the entire crop within a 3–4 week window, which is exactly what canners need — a predictable large harvest all at once rather than a continuous trickle. VF resistance (Verticillium and Fusarium wilt) keeps plants healthy through the season. For zone 9, plant in late January to early February so the harvest window falls in late April through May before summer heat stresses the plants. An outstanding choice for serious home canning enthusiasts who want a reliable, high-yield processing crop.
sources: H.J. Heinz Company seed breeding historical records
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tomato Production Guide
National Center for Home Food Preservation (USDA-NCHFP)

common_name: Lemon Boy Tomato (Bright Yellow Low-Acid Hybrid Tomato)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Lemon Boy is an indeterminate hybrid requiring staking or caging. The bright lemon-yellow fruits make a striking visual contrast in mixed tomato plantings and on plates.

directions: Start seeds 6–8 weeks before transplanting at 70–80°F; Lemon Boy is the best-known yellow tomato variety — an indeterminate hybrid that produces large (7–8 oz), bright canary-yellow, globe-shaped fruits with smooth, crack-resistant skin and mild, sweet, very low-acid flavor. The distinctive color combined with full tomato size makes Lemon Boy particularly useful for fresh slicing where visual presentation matters, contributing a sunny yellow contrast to caprese salads, tomato platters, and sandwiches. The low acid content makes Lemon Boy appealing to people with acid sensitivity who love tomatoes; the flavor is sweet and mild with less of the sharp acidity of red tomatoes, though some tasters find the flavor less complex. VFN disease resistance keeps plants productive. As an indeterminate variety, Lemon Boy produces continuously through the season and reaches 5–6 feet, requiring sturdy staking. An excellent choice for mixed color tomato plantings in zones 5–10 and a striking conversation piece in garden or market.
sources: Burpee Seed Company historical variety records
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tomato Production Guide
Oregon State University Extension — Growing Tomatoes

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Rutgers is an indeterminate variety that performs well with staking or caging and produces reliably in a wide range of climates from spring through early fall.

directions: Start seeds 6–8 weeks before transplanting at 70–80°F; Rutgers tomato is a historically significant American heirloom, developed in 1934 as a joint project between Rutgers University and the Campbell Soup Company, that became the dominant processing and fresh-market tomato in America from the 1940s through the 1970s — the reference standard for what a traditional American garden tomato should taste like. The medium-large (6–8 oz), slightly flattened, deep red, globe-shaped fruits have a firm but yielding texture with balanced sweet-acid traditional tomato flavor that is widely praised as a benchmark for real tomato taste in contrast to modern flavorless commercial varieties. Rutgers is neither the most disease-resistant nor the highest yielding tomato today, but it remains a beloved open-pollinated heirloom for gardeners who want authentic vintage tomato flavor, the ability to save seeds, and a piece of American agricultural history. The indeterminate plants reach 4–5 feet and benefit from staking; Rutgers is well-suited to the Northeast and mid-Atlantic where it was developed but grows well in most climates including the Gulf South in spring and fall seasons.
sources: Rutgers University New Jersey Agricultural Experiment Station historical variety records
Cornell Cooperative Extension — Heirloom Tomato Varieties

common_name: Viva Italia Tomato (Hybrid Roma Paste Tomato)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Viva Italia is a determinate paste tomato that ripens a concentrated crop over 3–4 weeks, ideal for sauce-making; in zone 9 aim for spring harvest in May–June before summer heat.

directions: Start seeds 6–8 weeks before transplanting at 70–80°F; Viva Italia is an improved hybrid roma-type paste tomato that addresses the main weakness of heirloom Roma — its susceptibility to disease. An AAS (All-America Selections) winner, Viva Italia combines the thick-walled, low-moisture flesh and rich, concentrated paste-tomato flavor of traditional Roma with VFFNTA disease resistance (Verticillium wilt, Fusarium wilt races 1 and 2, root-knot nematodes, tobacco mosaic virus, and Alternaria), making it far more reliable in humid climates and typical garden soils than open-pollinated Roma varieties. The determinate, compact (3–3.5 foot) plants produce large, concentrated sets of uniform, thick-skinned, 2–4 oz, elongated-oval, bright red fruits with very low moisture content and dense, meaty flesh — exactly what's needed for sauce, paste, sun-dried tomatoes, and canning. Because of the dense flesh and concentrated flavor, Viva Italia produces much higher yields of usable sauce per pound of fruit than wet, beefsteak-type tomatoes. An outstanding choice for serious sauce-makers who want disease resistance and reliability.
sources: All-America Selections — Viva Italia AAS winner
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tomato Production Guide
